corridor_bridge: The chasm opens before you. At this level there is a floor — you can walk across to the other side. The railing runs along the edge.
kiosk_intro: The kiosk glows softly. A panel, a slot. It gives anything you ask for — any food, any drink, any specificity. A vanilla shake from a shop you remember. A diet Coke. It appears likewise.
kiosk_dark: The kiosk is dark. It doesn't work when the lights are out.
bedroom_intro: Seven beds in a row, thin mattresses on metal frames. A bathroom through the far door. The room is identical to every other bedroom you've seen.
submission_intro: A slot in the wall, about the size of a mail slot. Brass edges, slightly worn. This is where you submit your book — if you've found it.
submission_fail: You slide the book in. A pause. It slides back out. Nothing happens.
win_release: The slot accepts the book. It does not come back.
win_through: Something shifts. The shelves, the corridor, the endless carpet — all of it thins, like a page held up to light.
win_light: You step through. The library is behind you. Whatever comes next, you are no longer there.
chasm_abyss: You stand at the railing. The two sides of the library converge below you into parallel lines, then a single line, then nothing. The bottom is not visible. The bottom may not exist.
chasm_deep: You stand at the railing. The floors below dwindle to threads. The far side mirrors yours, shrinking to a seam of light. You cannot see the bottom.
chasm_mid: You stand at the railing. Hundreds of floors are visible below, each one a thin bright line. Far down, the detail dissolves into haze. The bottom is somewhere beneath that.
chasm_low: You stand at the railing. You can see individual floors below — shelves, railings, the carpet edge. The bottom is visible, a long way down. The bridge crosses at the base.
chasm_near: You stand at the railing. The bottom is close. You can see the stone floor of the chasm, the bridge, people moving. A fall from here would hurt but you'd walk away.
death_mortality: Your body gives out. Thirst or hunger or both — in the end it doesn't matter which. You fall where you stand. The carpet is the last thing you feel.
death_gravity: You hit the bottom of the chasm. The impact is absolute. For a moment you are aware of every bone in your body, and then you are not aware of anything at all.
death_trauma: You reach for the railing. At this speed, reaching means colliding. Metal meets bone. The library spins. Then it stops.
resurrection: That strange hour before dawn. Sleep that can't be helped. All things repaired, made right and new. You wake. Your body is whole. The library is unchanged.
dead_npc_at_location: lies still on the carpet. They'll be back at dawn.
is crumpled against the shelves. Not breathing. It's temporary.
lies face-down. Tomorrow they'll wake, same as always.
darkness_corridor: The lights are out. Total darkness. Not even the whisper of any air movement. The stillness of the grave.
Dark. You navigate by touch — fingertips along the shelf edges. The silence is complete.
Pitch black. Your footsteps are the only sound in the world.
darkness_rest_area: The rest area is dark. The kiosk is off. The clock face is unreadable. Open space around you, felt but not seen.
Darkness. The beds are somewhere nearby. The open air of the chasm-side pulls faintly at your awareness.
darkness_no_read: You can't see. The book is in your hands but the pages are invisible.
despair_read_blocked: You reach for a book and your hand stops. You can't make yourself care what's inside.
You stare at the shelf. The spines blur together. You can't bring yourself to open one.
What's the point. They're all nonsense. Every one of them.
Your hand won't move. The books are there. You are not.
falling_abyss_slow: You fall. The two corridors stretch above and below in perfect parallels. Nothing changes. No sense of motion except the air beginning to move.
falling_abyss_fast: Wind. The corridors are lines now, converging above and below into vanishing points. No reference. No scale. Just falling between two infinities.
falling_deep_slow: Floors pass. You can make out the shape of each level — railing, shelves, railing — but they're small, distant. The bottom is not visible.
falling_deep_fast: The floors streak past in bands of light and dark. The wind pulls at your clothes. Above you the levels compress into a bright line. Below, the same.
falling_mid_slow: Floors drift past. Individual shelves are visible on each level. The structure of the library is clear from here — shelf, gap, shelf, gap, endlessly.
falling_mid_fast: The shelves blur into bands of brown and gold. Wind roars. The floors above compress into a narrowing column of light. Below, the detail starts to resolve.
falling_low_slow: The bottom is visible now. Stone floor, the bridge crossing. Floors pass slowly. You can read the spines on shelves as they go by.
falling_low_fast: The bottom rushes up. Shelves streak past. The stone floor of the chasm is visible below, growing fast.
falling_near_slow: Almost down. The bottom is right there. You can see the texture of the stone, the bridge supports, the railings on the lowest floors.
falling_near_fast: The ground. It's coming up fast.
falling_dark: Darkness. Falling. No way to know how fast, how far, how long. Just the wind and the absence of everything.
falling_grab_success: Your hands close on a railing. The impact wrenches your shoulders. You hold on.
falling_grab_fail: You reach for a railing. The impact tears through your arm. Your grip fails. The floor falls away again.
falling_grab_hopeless: The railings are a blur. You couldn't grab one if you tried.
falling_landed: You hit the bottom. The floor is solid stone. You lie still. Everything hurts, but everything works.
falling_landed_fatal: You hit the bottom. The impact is absolute.
sign_body: This Hell is based upon a short story by Jorge Luis Borges called “The Library of Babel.” Here you will find all the books that can possibly be written. Each book is 410 pages long, 40 lines of 80 characters — about 95 possible characters per position, for a total of 95^1,312,000 books.

When you are ready to leave, find the book describing your earthly life story (without errors, e.g., in spelling, grammar, etc.) and submit it through the slot below this sign. If the story is accepted, you will be admitted into a glorious heaven filled with wonders and joys beyond your imagination.
The food kiosk will provide whatever you would like to eat. Just ask for it.
We would ask that you please follow a few simple rules during your stay in Hell:
sign_rules: Please be kind. Treat others as you would like to be treated. Failure to do this will bring unhappiness and misery to you and your fellow citizens.
Do not get discouraged. Remember nothing lasts forever. Someday this will be a distant memory.
Please leave towels on the floor if you wish them to be cleaned. Hang up those you wish to use again.
Books not in your possession will be returned to their original place on the stacks every night. A book will be considered in your possession if you are touching it.
If you are killed you will be restored to life on the following day. Please try to avoid death as much as possible.

All contracts, bonds, commitments, covenants, pledges, and promises entered into prior to your entering Hell are null and void. This includes, but is not limited to: debt, marriage, natural births and adoptions, requirements of citizenship, military obligations, student loans, etc.
Remember you are never really alone. Although it may feel like it for very long stretches of time.
Please don’t write on, mark, or mar library materials. Although repairs are made nightly, we would like to keep repairs to a minimum.
Lastly, you are here to learn something. Don’t try to figure out what it is. This can be frustrating and unproductive.
sign_closing: We hope you enjoy your stay here. We have done all we can to make your stay a pleasant and instructive one.
menu_new_game_confirm: Are you sure? All progress will be lost.
memory_witness_chasm: You saw someone fall into the chasm.
memory_found_body: You found someone dead on the floor.
memory_found_words: You found legible words in a book.
memory_witness_madness: You watched someone lose their mind.
memory_companion_mad: Someone you were close to went mad.
ambient_memory_chasm: The railing catches your eye. You think of what you saw.
You pass the edge of the chasm and look away.
The drop is familiar. Not in a good way.
ambient_memory_body: The carpet looks ordinary. You remember when it didn't.
You half-expect to find someone here again.
ambient_memory_escape: Someone left from somewhere near here. You remember the feeling.
You think of what it looked like when they went through.
ambient_memory_madness: Something happened here. You can still hear it.
The shelf is normal. Nothing about it is normal to you.
